Design System
🎨Design System
Relowa POC · v0.1
Home / ADR-0004

ADR-0004 — Multi-Agent Orchestration

StatusAccepted
Date2026-05-09

Context

Relowa is built collaboratively with AI agents (opencode, Claude Code, Cursor). A solo lead operating without a multi-agent workflow leaves significant leverage on the table:

  • Different parts of the work benefit from different specializations — DB schema design, RLS policy authorship, integration testing, frontend work, documentation.
  • Without explicit roles, every session reinvents context, costs more tokens, and produces inconsistent quality.
  • Without a memory system, lessons learned in session 5 are forgotten in session 12.

The goal is structured multi-agent workflow with specialist roles, consistent context, and compounding institutional knowledge.

Decision

We adopt a three-tier multi-agent orchestration model:

Tier 1 — Lead session (orchestrator). Single human-facing session. Routes work to specialist sessions. Reads AGENTS.md + CHANGELOG.md at start of every session.

Tier 2 — Specialist skills. Defined as .opencode/skills/*.md. Each skill is a self-contained Markdown file describing when to invoke it, expected inputs/outputs, and constraints.

Tier 3 — Memory system. docs/memory/ as an Obsidian knowledge graph. Cross-linked with wikilinks so agents and humans can traverse naturally.

Initial Specialist Skills

Skill Purpose
migration-author Writes new Drizzle schema + raw SQL side-car migrations with RLS policies.
rls-test-runner Executes the RLS isolation suite, reports failures with clear diffs.
audit-trail-verifier Validates the audit hash chain integrity, surfaces tampering attempts.
endpoint-writer Writes Hono API endpoints with idempotency middleware and JWT GUC setup.
realtime-debugger Diagnoses Supabase Realtime / logical replication issues in dev and staging.
doc-keeper Maintains the memory graph in docs/memory/, writes learned notes and micro-decisions.

Memory System Layout

Directory Contains Lifespan
concepts/ Long-lived concept explainers (auth pattern, multi-tenancy, idempotency, hash chain) Permanent
decisions/ Short notes capturing micro-decisions that don't merit full ADRs Permanent
learned/ Gotchas, pitfalls, surprising failure modes encountered in development Permanent (review quarterly)

Consequences

▲ Positive
  • Sessions get fresh context fast — read AGENTS.md + relevant skill file.
  • Lessons learned compound — learned/ notes mean the same trap is sprung at most once.
  • Future sessions (or future contributors) inherit institutional knowledge without ceremony.
  • Skills can be reused across projects with minimal modification.
▼ Negative
  • Documentation discipline cost. Mitigated by making PR reviews include relevant learned/ or decisions/ notes.
  • Skills risk staleness. Mitigated by quarterly retro review of all skill files.
  • Agents may misinterpret scope. Mitigated by explicit non-goals sections in each skill file.

How Agents Interact With This System

  1. Start of session: read AGENTS.md, scan CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased], and scan recent learned/ notes for relevant gotchas.
  2. Matching a skill: when asked to do work that fits a skill, load the skill file into context and follow its directives.
  3. Discovering a gotcha: write a learned/ note immediately — before continuing the work — so the lesson isn't lost.
  4. Making a decision: if non-trivial and architectural, write a full ADR. If small and scoped, write a decisions/ note.
  5. End of session: update CHANGELOG.md under [Unreleased] with what changed and why.

Validation

This ADR is the foundational record for multi-agent collaboration. The skill files in .opencode/skills/ and the memory directory in docs/memory/ are its operational manifestation. Compliance is measured by PR review — every PR should reference either a skill invocation or a memory note.

See Also
